skill.name: planning-framework
skill.description: Apply structured planning before coding with requirement questioning, simplification, ICE scoring, and implementation checklists.
skill.summary: Plan coding work with requirement checks, simplification, ICE scoring, and concise implementation plans.
skill.author: 5MinFutures

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported Ruby or shell backticks, weak cryptography, and system reconnaissance patterns in SKILL.md. Manual review found these are Markdown code examples and ordinary prose, with no executable scripts, network activity, environment access, prompt injection, or data exfiltration behavior.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Command Execution
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The reported backticks are Markdown inline code spans, fenced TypeScript examples, or example npm and TypeScript commands inside a planning guide. No evidence found of automatic shell execution, dynamic command construction, or user input passed to a command runner.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The only scanned file is SKILL.md, and the flagged lines are rendered documentation examples. There are no scripts or executable source files that could run these snippets automatically.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Pattern Misclassified from Description Text
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The static weak cryptography alerts point to the frontmatter description line. No evidence found of cryptographic functions, hashing APIs, encryption logic, or credential handling in the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 is plain descriptive metadata for a planning skill. The duplicate static alerts appear to match text fragments rather than real cryptographic usage.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Pattern Misclassified from Project Lessons
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. Lines 338 and 339 describe project decisions about Redux and chart libraries. No evidence found of host inspection, process listing, filesystem probing, or environment discovery.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are human-readable retrospective notes. They do not contain commands, APIs, or instructions that collect system information.

content.user_title: Plan Coding Work With Structured Frameworks
content.value_statement: Complex coding work can start without clear priorities, causing avoidable rework. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through questioning, deletion, ICE scoring, and concise implementation plans.
actual_capabilities: Guides requirement questioning before significant coding work.
Applies a five-step process for deleting, simplifying, accelerating, and automating work.
Uses ICE scoring to compare feature, refactor, and testing priorities.
Provides planning checklists for scope, affected files, edge cases, and time estimates.
Highlights common planning and implementation red flags.
limitations: Does not inspect a repository or calculate scores without user-provided context.
Does not execute commands or enforce architectural decisions automatically.
Uses examples from one project, so users should adapt them to their codebase.
Does not replace security, legal, or product approval for high-risk changes.

use_cases.title: Scope a New Feature
use_cases.description: Question the request, identify simpler options, and produce a small implementation plan before coding starts.

use_cases.title: Prioritize Technical Debt
use_cases.description: Compare refactoring candidates with ICE scoring and choose work that improves maintainability without delaying urgent features.

use_cases.title: Plan a Risky Refactor
use_cases.description: Break a large component change into extracted parts, test points, risks, and a realistic time estimate.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Small Change
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner prompt for a contained feature or bug fix.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the planning framework for this change: [describe change]. Question the requirement, list what can be removed, and give a 3-step plan.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Implementation Options
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate prompt for choosing between approaches.
prompt_templates.prompt: Evaluate these approaches with ICE scoring: [option A], [option B], [option C]. Explain the scores and recommend one path.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare a Refactor Plan
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced prompt for restructuring existing code.
prompt_templates.prompt: Apply the framework to refactor [component or module]. Identify deletion opportunities, extraction points, risks, tests, and a realistic time estimate.

prompt_templates.title: Review an Architecture Decision
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced prompt for larger design choices.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the five-step framework to challenge this architecture proposal: [proposal]. Find assumptions, simpler alternatives, automation timing, and decision criteria.

output_examples.input: Plan adding export support to a metrics table.
output: Question: Users need exports for offline analysis, but only table data is required first.
Delete: Skip advanced formatting and scheduled exports for the first version.
Plan: Add one export action, reuse existing table data, test filtered and empty states.
Estimate: Two to four hours, depending on file format requirements.

output_examples.input: Choose between adding tests, refactoring a large component, or building a minor visual feature.
output: Highest priority: Add tests for critical calculations because impact and confidence are high.
Second priority: Refactor the large component after tests reduce regression risk.
Lowest priority: Delay the visual feature unless users explicitly need it.

output_examples.input: Review a proposal to automate deployment immediately.
output: Challenge: Deployment frequency and failure cost are not yet clear.
Simpler path: Document the manual release checklist first.
Decision: Automate after repeated manual steps are proven necessary.

best_practices: Provide the user request, affected code areas, constraints, and known risks before asking for a plan.
Use ICE scoring for choices that compete for time, not for every small task.
Ask for a short implementation plan before coding begins on multi-file changes.
anti_patterns: Do not use the framework to justify work after the implementation is already chosen.
Do not automate a process before proving the manual workflow is repeated and stable.
Do not treat example scores as universal; rescore them with your project context.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps structure coding plans before implementation, especially for features, refactors, architecture choices, and prioritization.

faq.question: Does it write code?
faq.answer: It can support coding discussions, but its main value is planning, scoring options, and reducing unnecessary work before coding.

faq.question: What is ICE scoring?
faq.answer: ICE scoring compares options using impact, confidence, and ease so teams can prioritize work more consistently.

faq.question: Can it be used with Codex?
faq.answer: Yes. It is compatible with Codex, Claude, and Claude Code as a planning guide before implementation.

faq.question: Is the framework only for large projects?
faq.answer: No. It is most useful for significant work, but the checklist can also clarify smaller tasks with unclear requirements.

faq.question: Does it run commands on my machine?
faq.answer: No. The skill is documentation-based and does not include scripts or automatic command execution.
